summary: Hotz argues that humans are fundamentally predictable systems, just with trillions of parameters instead of simple equations, and that this realization from studying machine learning reveals how vulnerable we are to manipulation. He warns that AI and targeted advertising are forms of 'wireheading' that threaten human free will and autonomy. The post criticizes Silicon Valley's lack of humanities education while wielding unprecedented power over human behavior. He concludes that current AI won't end humanity directly, but could trigger a collapse of machine civilization, leaving only the 'street people' and ushering in a new dark age.
key_points: Free will is an illusion created by the 'veil of computability' - behavior looks random until you find the pattern
Humans are predictable like any other system, just with 100 trillion parameters instead of two
Targeted advertising and AI manipulation are actively eroding human autonomy and should not be culturally acceptable
Silicon Valley has immense power but lacks humanities education to understand people
Machines depend on humans for survival but not vice versa - if machines died, humanity continues; if humans died, machines would shortly follow
money_quotes: The dreams of my childhood have come true, studying machine learning has shown me how I work.
AI is coming and we are so unbelievably unprepared.
The amount of power over nature that the Silicon Valley death cult is stumbling into is horrifying, and these high priests don't have a basic grasp of people.
This will not stop until they take our free will from us.
Nukes don't end humanity. Current path AI doesn't end humanity. It just ends all the machines and hands the world over to the street people.
Now I see how the dark ages happened.
If all the humans died today, all the machines would shortly follow. If all the machines died today, humanity would keep on going.
key_insight: Understanding machine learning reveals that human behavior is just as mechanistic and predictable as any algorithm, making us vulnerable to manipulation at scale - and the tech industry is exploiting this without understanding the civilizational consequences.
